topic.title: Eigenvectors and PCA
topic.question: Why are PCA's principal components the eigenvectors of the data covariance matrix, ranked by eigenvalue?

lesson.title: Why PCA Components Are Eigenvectors of the Covariance Matrix
lesson.payoff: The direction u that maximizes variance in projected data equals the eigenvector of C with the largest eigenvalue lambda, capturing lambda units of variance
staircase: Variance as the quantity PCA maximizes
The covariance matrix C encodes all pairwise spread
Projection of data onto a unit vector u
Projected variance as a quadratic form u-transpose C u
Maximizing a quadratic form subject to unit-norm constraint via Lagrange multipliers
The critical-point equation is exactly the eigenvector equation C u equals lambda u
Ranking by eigenvalue ranks by captured variance
lesson.destination: The learner can derive that maximizing projected variance subject to unit norm yields the eigenvector equation C u = lambda u, and that eigenvalues equal the variances captured by each principal component

narration.fullText: Our goal today is to answer one precise question: why does PCA find eigenvectors of the covariance matrix, and why do larger eigenvalues mean more important directions? Let us build the answer from scratch. PCA stands for Principal Component Analysis. Its single objective is to find a direction in the data space that captures as much variance as possible. Variance here means spread: we want the data, once projected onto our chosen direction, to be as spread out as it can be. Now suppose we have n data points, each a column vector in R-d, meaning d-dimensional real space. We center the data first, meaning we subtract the mean so that the average of all points is the zero vector. Call the centered data matrix X, where each column is one data point. The covariance matrix C is defined as X times X-transpose divided by n. C is a d-by-d symmetric matrix, and its entry in row i column j measures how much dimension i and dimension j vary together across the dataset. Now we choose a direction. A direction is represented by a unit vector u in R-d, where unit means u-transpose u equals one. We project every data point onto u by computing u-transpose times each column of X. The projected dataset is then the row vector u-transpose X. The variance of those projected scalars is the average of their squared values, which equals u-transpose X times X-transpose u divided by n. But X X-transpose over n is exactly C. So projected variance equals u-transpose C u. This expression, u-transpose C u, is called a quadratic form in u. PCA asks: which unit vector u maximizes this quadratic form? We enforce the unit-norm constraint u-transpose u equals one using a Lagrange multiplier lambda. We write the Lagrangian L equals u-transpose C u minus lambda times the quantity u-transpose u minus one. Setting the gradient of L with respect to u to zero gives two times C u minus two times lambda u equals zero, which simplifies to C u equals lambda u. That is the eigenvector equation. A vector u satisfying C u equals lambda u is called an eigenvector of C, and the scalar lambda is its eigenvalue. So any critical point of projected variance must be an eigenvector of C. To find the maximum, we evaluate projected variance at any eigenvector: u-transpose C u equals u-transpose times lambda u equals lambda times u-transpose u equals lambda times one equals lambda. The projected variance equals the eigenvalue. Therefore the direction that maximizes variance is the eigenvector with the largest eigenvalue, capturing exactly lambda-one units of variance. The second principal component is the eigenvector with the second largest eigenvalue, orthogonal to the first, capturing lambda-two units of variance, and so on. The full ranking of principal components is exactly the ranking of eigenvectors by eigenvalue, largest to smallest.

beats.text: Our goal today is to answer one precise question: why does PCA find eigenvectors of the covariance matrix, and why do larger eigenvalues mean more important directions?
beats.whyThisBeat: Payoff: state the destination so the learner knows what they are climbing toward

beats.text: PCA stands for Principal Component Analysis. Its single objective is to find a direction in the data space that captures as much variance as possible. Variance here means spread: we want the data, once projected onto our chosen direction, to be as spread out as it can be.
beats.whyThisBeat: Rung 1: establish variance as the quantity being maximized

beats.text: Suppose we have n data points, each a column vector in R-d, meaning d-dimensional real space. We center the data first, subtracting the mean so the average of all points is the zero vector. Call the centered data matrix X, where each column is one data point.
beats.whyThisBeat: Setup: introduce centered data matrix X so later formulas have grounding

beats.text: The covariance matrix C is defined as X times X-transpose divided by n. C is a d-by-d symmetric matrix, and its entry in row i column j measures how much dimension i and dimension j vary together.
beats.whyThisBeat: Rung 2: define C and explain what it encodes

beats.text: Now we choose a direction: a unit vector u in R-d, where unit means u-transpose u equals one. We project every data point onto u by computing u-transpose times each column of X.
beats.whyThisBeat: Rung 3: projection of data onto a unit vector

beats.text: The variance of those projected scalars equals u-transpose X times X-transpose u divided by n. But X X-transpose over n is exactly C. So projected variance equals u-transpose C u.
beats.whyThisBeat: Rung 4: projected variance is the quadratic form u-transpose C u

beats.text: PCA asks: which unit vector u maximizes u-transpose C u subject to u-transpose u equals one? We use a Lagrange multiplier lambda. The Lagrangian is L equals u-transpose C u minus lambda times the quantity u-transpose u minus one.
beats.whyThisBeat: Rung 5: set up the constrained optimization with a Lagrange multiplier

beats.text: Setting the gradient of L with respect to u to zero gives C u equals lambda u. That is the eigenvector equation. A vector u satisfying this is called an eigenvector of C; the scalar lambda is its eigenvalue.
beats.whyThisBeat: Rung 6: the critical-point equation is exactly the eigenvector equation

beats.text: To find which eigenvector wins, evaluate projected variance at any eigenvector: u-transpose C u equals u-transpose lambda u equals lambda. The projected variance equals the eigenvalue itself.
beats.whyThisBeat: Key insight: eigenvalue equals variance captured, so the largest eigenvalue identifies the best direction

beats.text: Therefore the direction maximizing variance is the eigenvector with the largest eigenvalue, capturing lambda-one units of variance. The second principal component is the eigenvector with the second largest eigenvalue, capturing lambda-two units, and so on.
beats.whyThisBeat: Rung 7: ranking eigenvectors by eigenvalue ranks them by captured variance

beats.text: The full ranking of principal components is exactly the ranking of eigenvectors by eigenvalue, largest to smallest. That is why PCA decomposes to the eigenvectors of C, ordered by eigenvalue.
beats.whyThisBeat: Destination: restate the complete mechanism as a single crisp conclusion

actions.content: Why are PCA components eigenvectors of the covariance matrix?
PCA goal: find direction u maximizing variance of projected data
n data points in R^d, centered: mean = 0  Data matrix X (d x n), each column = one point
Covariance matrix: C = (1/n) X X^T  C is d x d, symmetric  C_{ij} = how much dim i and dim j co-vary
Direction: unit vector u in R^d  Constraint: u^T u = 1
Projected data: u^T X  (row of scalars)
Projected variance = (1/n) (u^T X)(u^T X)^T = (1/n) u^T X X^T u = u^T C u
Maximize u^T C u subject to u^T u = 1  Lagrangian: L = u^T C u - lambda(u^T u - 1)
grad_u L = 0  => 2 C u - 2 lambda u = 0
u = eigenvector of C  lambda = eigenvalue of C
Projected variance at eigenvector u: u^T C u = u^T (lambda u) = lambda (u^T u) = lambda
lambda_1 >= lambda_2 >= ... >= lambda_d  PC1 = eigenvec for lambda_1  (most variance)  PC2 = eigenvec for lambda_2  (next most)
Principal components = eigenvectors of C  Ranked by eigenvalue = ranked by variance captured
eigenvectors of C, ranked by eigenvalue
